exemplar.title:
To what extent has tourism impacted the development and 
services offered in Girona, Spain?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent has tourism impacted the development and 
services offered in Girona, Spain?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is precise, geographical and grounded in syllabus theory with testable hypotheses
Background provides detailed locational context, transport times and historical evolution ensuring feasibility
Clear link to Option G and UN-Habitatâs definition situates the study within geographical theory
weaknesses: Some map figures (e.g. basemap and sampling-area map) lack clarity and complete conventions (legends, resolution)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The formulation of the fieldwork question and its theoretical grounding are exemplary (two strands at band 3). The geographic context is detailed and feasible for primary data collection (band 3). Minor mapping inconsistencies (resolution, missing legend) prevent perfection, but the overall performance clearly aligns with band 3.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (Tourist Service Index, dice sampling) are described with clear relevance to the research question
Ethical and safety considerations are explicitly identified
weaknesses: Rationale for sample size and formula choice is brief and lacks theoretical justification
Reliability checks (e.g. inter-observer consistency) are not addressed, and sample size is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method descriptions and data collection techniques are correctly implemented and linked to the research question, meeting the band 2 descriptor. However, the justification is not fully developed and data robustness is limited, so it does not reach band 3.

strengths: All collected data are directly relevant to the research question
Appropriate presentation techniques (bar graphs, maps, photos) aid interpretation
weaknesses: Data set is quantitatively thin and insufficient for inâdepth analysis
Formatting errors in tables and inconsistent map conventions (legends, symbols) hinder clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the data are relevant and presentation methods are generally appropriate, the small sample size and recurring formatting/convention errors limit sufficiency and adherence. This aligns with the top of the band 1â2 descriptor.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive statistical techniques are applied appropriately
Trends and anomalies are identified and linked to the research question and theory
weaknesses: Analysis lacks advanced statistical measures (e.g. confidence intervals, error bars)
Evidence gaps remain (limited quantitative error analysis)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses suitable descriptive and basic statistical analysis and links findings to theory, fulfilling band 2. However, missing statistical depth and incomplete evidence prevent placement in a higher band.

strengths: Conclusions are clear, directly reference analysis and hypotheses
Fully supported by the presented evidence
weaknesses: Does not quantify or discuss how sample bias might affect validity of conclusions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion succinctly answers the research question and is well supported, meeting the band 2 criteria. A more nuanced discussion of data limitations would strengthen the overall argument.

strengths: Comprehensive discussion of methodological strengths and weaknesses with clear impacts on validity
Detailed and relevant improvement suggestions (e.g. larger teams, inter-observer checks, broader stakeholder surveys) with explained benefits
weaknesses: Cost and logistical constraints of proposed improvements are not discussed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation offers deep reflection on data collection methods and presents multiple detailed improvements with clear impact, satisfying the top-band descriptor for band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three maps are provided (Spain, city, sampling area) with north arrow and scale bars, but figure clarity is uneven (blurred basemap, unclear symbols) and the sampling-area map lacks a full key. Mapping conventions are present but not fully consistent, matching the middle band of âappropriate but imperfectâ.

The research question is clearly geographical, focused on the impact of tourism on urban areas, and explicitly situated within the Option G syllabus theme of urban regeneration. The student links the study to UN-Habitatâs definition of regeneration, cites syllabus pages, and generates two testable hypotheses. This shows theoretical grounding and allows for hypothesis formation, meeting the top-band descriptor.

Bar graphs, annotated photos and colour-coded maps are appropriate for the variables studied and aid interpretation. While effective, the visual quality is mixed (hand-annotated scans, blurred maps), preventing placement in the top band; overall performance fits the 3â4 range at 3 marks.

Techniques (store tally, dice-guided transects) are correctly implemented and yield primary data, but only 40 services and eight opinion points limit robustness. Ethical and safety notes are included, yet reliability checks are minimal; therefore, quality is adequate but not high, meriting band 2.

The student calculates TSI and simple averages, discusses skew and error qualitatively, and comments on sample size. These are appropriate descriptive/statistical techniques but lack confidence intervals or advanced tests, positioning the work solidly in the 3â4 band at 3 marks.

Trends (higher TSI, positive opinions northward) are identified and linked to urban regeneration theory and the hypotheses. Anomalies (dirty river) are mentioned. The linkage is clear but not exhaustive, meeting the 3â4 band expectations at 3 marks.

The submission gives a concise background on Girona (population, historical context, regeneration history) and justifies the choice of two contrasting study areas (Old Quarter vs. Mercadel). Detailed travel times and historical context confirm feasibility for primary data collection, indicating a precise, well-defined location.

Strengths (relevant methods, random sampling) and weaknesses (small sample, bias) are discussed with explanations of their impact on reliability. The evaluation shows insight into how these factors affect validity, matching the comprehensive descriptor.

The data collected are relevant but quantitatively thin (two TSI scores per zone, eight attractiveness scores). The student acknowledges the small sample and potential bias, showing partial sufficiency rather than comprehensive coverage; this aligns with the top of the 1â2 band.

The student states conclusions for each hypothesis and references supporting averages and map patterns, providing clear alignment with earlier analysis. Although not deeply nuanced, the conclusions are fully supported by the presented evidence, warranting the top band (2 marks) for Criterion E.

Both hypotheses are addressed, but limitations in data depth leave gaps (e.g., lack of quantitative error analysis). Thus, analysis is competent with some omissions, fitting the middle band (3 marks).

Methods (Tourist Service Index, random dice sampling) are described in moderate detail and clearly relate to the question, but justification is brief and the rationale for sample size is weak. The explanation of the TSI formula shows relevance yet lacks comprehensive theoretical alignment, fitting the band 2 descriptor.

Most figures include titles, north arrow and scale, but some omit source or key details and figure numbering is occasionally inconsistent. These are âoccasionalâ rather than frequent errors, matching the middle band (3 marks).

Multiple, detailed improvement suggestions are provided (larger mixed-gender teams, checking unsigned premises, surveying locals) and the student explains how each would enhance reliability or breadth of data. The depth and relevance satisfy the top band for this strand.

annotations.comment: The research question is precise, focused on tourismâs impact in Girona, and sets up clear hypotheses, demonstrating a strong geographical inquiry.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The background on Girona provides clear locational details, population data, and historical context, ensuring feasibility for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Fig. 1âs basemap appears blurred, reducing clarity. Improve resolution and ensure map conventions (north arrow, scale bar) are legible.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Fig. 2 omits a clear scale bar and legend. Add these elements to adhere fully to mapping conventions and aid reader interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographical background is well developed, detailing transport times and historic evolution to justify site selection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to Option G and UN-Habitatâs definition of urban regeneration effectively situates the study within syllabus theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypotheses are specific, testable, and logically derived from the research question and theory, enabling clear evaluation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Method description outlines use of TSI and dice sampling but lacks theoretical justification for sample size and formula choice.
Random sampling procedure is described clearly, but conducting only five trials may limit robustness; justify how this produces reliable results.
Ethical and safety considerations are noted, reflecting good practice in fieldwork protocols.
The sampling-area map lacks a full key explaining symbols and sampling points. Include a legend to follow mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: TSI data tables are cluttered with incomplete entries and formatting errors, hindering clarity; ensure full data and consistent layout.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Acknowledgement of potential bias in service categorization and steps taken to mitigate it demonstrate critical reflection on data treatment.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretation of TSI differences is directly linked to the research question, showing clear analytical thinking.
Sampling path description is thorough, yet no mention is made of inter-observer reliability; include checks for consistency in data collection.
The bar graph clearly shows average TSI values and acknowledges skew, reflecting appropriate descriptive statistics.
Bar graph lacks error bars or measures of variability; adding confidence intervals would strengthen statistical interpretation.
Fig. 10âs random sampling map omits a legend for color-coded ratings, making interpretation difficult; include a clear key.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Fig. 11 uses different symbols than Fig. 10, reducing comparability; standardize symbol conventions across maps.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Discussion comparing two groupsâ methods shows strong critical awareness of methodological strengths and weaknesses.
The narrative on sensory and environmental factors considered in opinion sampling enriches data quality and interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Spatial interpretation of opinion patterns effectively links observed trends to urban regeneration dynamics.
Inference about a âbleed effectâ on Mercadel is insightful but would benefit from external data or literature support.
Conclusion for Hypothesis 1 succinctly references average TSI values and aligns with analysis to answer the research question.
Hypothesis 2 conclusion notes group size disparity but does not quantify its effect on validity; elaborate on how this bias may skew results.
Evaluation offers detailed strategies to improve data collection reliability, demonstrating deep methodological reflection.
Proposal to survey local residents and in-person travel agencies broadens the evaluation and adds valuable stakeholder perspectives.
